subject: Geography
trap_factor: Earth's rotation (Coriolis) is a common distractor since it drives most major current systems. The convergence explanation (option B) is less intuitive.
elimination_hint: The equatorial counter-current flows eastward between the two westward-flowing equatorial currents — it is fed by the convergence/piling up of water at the western ocean margins.
factual_hooks: Equatorial counter-current flows eastward between North and South equatorial currents
Convergence at western ocean margins drives return flow eastward
contrast_with: North/South Equatorial Currents flow westward driven by trade winds
contemporary_relevance: Understanding ocean current patterns is important for climate science and El Niño/La Niña prediction.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Omkareshwar (Madhya Pradesh) is commonly confused with Satmala Hills (Maharashtra); Pushkar (Rajasthan) is in the Aravalli foothills, not Mahadeo Hills.
elimination_hint: Only Srisailam in Nallamala Hills (Andhra Pradesh) is correctly matched; eliminate all options containing pairs 2 or 3.
factual_hooks: Srisailam is located in Nallamala Hills, Andhra Pradesh
Omkareshwar is in Madhya Pradesh on Mandhata island, not Satmala Hills
Pushkar is in Aravalli hills area of Rajasthan
contrast_with: Satmala Hills are in Maharashtra not linked to Omkareshwar

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 3 is a common trap — Simon Commission protests (1928) and Rowlatt Satyagraha (1919) are separated by nearly a decade; students confuse major nationalist events.
elimination_hint: Rowlatt Act (1919) was based on Sedition Committee recommendations — statement 1 is correct. Gandhi used Home Rule League networks — statement 2 is correct. Statement 3 is factually wrong (Simon Commission 1928).
factual_hooks: Rowlatt Act 1919 based on Sedition Committee (Rowlatt Committee) report
Gandhi used Home Rule League infrastructure for Rowlatt Satyagraha
Simon Commission protests were in 1928, not coinciding with Rowlatt Satyagraha
contrast_with: Rowlatt Satyagraha 1919 vs Simon Commission protests 1928 — different events
contemporary_relevance: Rowlatt Act as a model of draconian legislation is debated in civil liberties contexts.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse Ebola with other disease outbreaks in different regions; the 2014 West Africa outbreak was the largest ever.
elimination_hint: The 2014 Ebola epidemic was centred in West Africa — Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia are the three most affected countries; eliminate all other regional options.
factual_hooks: 2014 Ebola epidemic: Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia were the worst hit
West Africa was the epicentre, not Central Africa where earlier outbreaks occurred
contrast_with: Earlier Ebola outbreaks were in Central Africa (DRC, Sudan)
contemporary_relevance: Ebola re-emerges periodically; pandemic preparedness remains relevant.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 is a classic trap — dugong is NOT found along the entire coast but mainly in the Gulf of Mannar and Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
elimination_hint: Dugong is a herbivorous marine mammal (grazes on seagrass) — statement 1 correct. It has restricted coastal distribution — statement 2 wrong. It is in Schedule I of WPA 1972 — statement 3 correct. So answer is 1 and 3.
factual_hooks: Dugong is a herbivorous marine mammal feeding on seagrass
Found mainly in Gulf of Mannar and Andaman & Nicobar, not entire coast
Schedule I of Wildlife Protection Act 1972 gives highest protection
related_concepts: Gulf of Mannar Marine National Park
contrast_with: Dugong vs manatee — both sirenians but different regions
contemporary_relevance: Dugong conservation is linked to Palk Bay and Gulf of Mannar marine ecosystem protection.

subject: History
factual_hooks: Colonial mentality refers to internalized inferiority from colonial rule
Post-colonial theorists like Fanon and Nandy explored this concept for India
contrast_with: Colonial mentality vs. nationalist self-reliance movements
mains_intro: Colonialism imposed not just economic extraction but a mental framework of inferiority that continues to shape India's developmental trajectory.
mains_body_points: Manifestations: preference for Western models, brain drain, neglect of indigenous knowledge
Examples of colonial mentality in bureaucracy, education, and urban planning
Counter-arguments: India's software success, space programme, diplomatic assertions
Need for balance between global integration and civilizational confidence
mains_conclusion: Shedding colonial mentality requires reimagining success on India's own developmental terms while engaging globally.
contemporary_relevance: Debate on Atmanirbhar Bharat and vocal for local reflects this theme.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students often miss G. Subramania Iyer; Dadabhai Naoroji and R.C. Dutt are well known but Iyer's economic critique is less prominent in standard texts.
elimination_hint: All three — Dadabhai Naoroji (drain of wealth), G. Subramania Iyer, and R.C. Dutt (Economic History of India) — were economic critics of colonialism; the answer is all three.
factual_hooks: Dadabhai Naoroji's 'Drain of Wealth' theory was foundational economic critique
R.C. Dutt wrote 'Economic History of India' critiquing British policies
G. Subramania Iyer was a journalist-economist who criticized colonial economic policies
contrast_with: Political critics vs. economic critics of colonialism

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: ADB also publishes economic outlooks which may cause confusion; students may mix up 'Global Economic Prospects' with 'World Economic Outlook' (IMF).
elimination_hint: The World Bank publishes 'Global Economic Prospects' twice a year; IMF publishes 'World Economic Outlook' — these are distinct reports.
factual_hooks: World Bank publishes 'Global Economic Prospects'
IMF publishes 'World Economic Outlook' — different report
prerequisite_concepts: International financial institutions and their publications
contrast_with: World Bank Global Economic Prospects vs IMF World Economic Outlook

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Essential Commodities Act is frequently associated with agricultural markets regulation, making option A an attractive wrong choice.
elimination_hint: Agricultural produce markets (mandis) are regulated under APMC Acts enacted by state governments; the Essential Commodities Act deals with supply and price control of specific commodities.
factual_hooks: APMC Acts are state subjects — states enact their own Agricultural Produce Market Committee laws
Essential Commodities Act deals with supply/price, not mandi regulation
contrast_with: APMC Acts (state) vs Essential Commodities Act (central)
contemporary_relevance: APMC reform was central to the contentious Farm Laws 2020 debate.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Gharial and saltwater crocodile are both protected aquatic animals leading students to pick them; Gangetic dolphin is specifically designated as national aquatic animal.
elimination_hint: The Gangetic dolphin (Platanista gangetica) was declared India's national aquatic animal in 2009; gharial and crocodile are reptiles, not aquatic mammals.
factual_hooks: Gangetic dolphin declared India's National Aquatic Animal in 2009
Also known as 'susu' — found in Ganga river system
Classified as endangered
contrast_with: National aquatic animal (Gangetic dolphin) vs national reptile (king cobra) vs national marine mammal (proposed)
contemporary_relevance: Project Dolphin launched 2020 to conserve Gangetic and Irrawaddy dolphins.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 3 is a trap — Rajya Sabha CAN discuss the Annual Financial Statement (Budget) even though it cannot vote on Demands for Grants; students often mark all three as correct.
elimination_hint: Rajya Sabha cannot amend/reject Money Bills (only recommend) — statement 1 correct. Cannot vote on Demands for Grants — statement 2 correct. But CAN discuss Annual Financial Statement — statement 3 wrong. Answer: 1 and 2 only.
factual_hooks: Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments to Money Bills, cannot reject them
Rajya Sabha cannot vote on Demands for Grants — exclusive Lok Sabha power
Rajya Sabha CAN discuss the Annual Financial Statement
prerequisite_concepts: Role of Rajya Sabha in budget
contrast_with: Rajya Sabha powers vs Lok Sabha powers on financial matters

subject: Economy
trap_factor: RBI is associated with many economic indices; students may confuse CPI-IW with other price indices compiled by different bodies.
elimination_hint: The Labour Bureau (under Ministry of Labour) compiles CPI for Industrial Workers; RBI uses CPI-Combined for monetary policy; CSO compiles National Income.
factual_hooks: Labour Bureau compiles CPI for Industrial Workers (CPI-IW)
CPI-IW is used for wage revision and dearness allowance calculations
contrast_with: CPI-IW (Labour Bureau) vs CPI-Combined (MOSPI/CSO) vs WPI (DPIIT)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think a neutrino detector and dark matter telescope are mutually exclusive; IceCube serves both purposes which makes all three statements correct.
elimination_hint: IceCube is the world's largest neutrino detector (1 cubic km of ice, buried deep) and also used to search for dark matter signatures — all three statements are correct.
factual_hooks: IceCube Neutrino Observatory: 1 cubic km of instrumented ice at South Pole
Used for both neutrino detection and dark matter search
Located 1.5–2.5 km deep in Antarctic ice
contrast_with: IceCube (ice-based) vs Super-Kamiokande (water-based) neutrino detectors

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: FAO deals with food and agriculture making it a tempting wrong answer; the Agreement on Agriculture and Peace Clause are specifically WTO instruments.
elimination_hint: Agreement on Agriculture, Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures, and Peace Clause are all WTO agreements governing agricultural trade; FAO handles food security not trade rules.
factual_hooks: Agreement on Agriculture and Peace Clause are WTO instruments from Uruguay Round
Peace Clause protects developing countries' food security subsidies under WTO
SPS Agreement sets standards for food safety and animal/plant health in trade
contrast_with: FAO (food security institution) vs WTO (trade rules institution)
contemporary_relevance: India's food subsidy program has been a contentious WTO issue under the Peace Clause.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the trap — NFC works only at very short range (up to 4 cm, not 1 metre); students unfamiliar with NFC specs may accept the 1-metre figure.
elimination_hint: NFC uses electromagnetic radio fields (correct) and supports encryption (correct), but works only at very close range (~4 cm), not 1 metre — so statements 1 and 3 are correct.
factual_hooks: NFC operates at 13.56 MHz using electromagnetic induction
NFC range is typically up to 4 cm, not 1 metre
NFC supports encryption for secure transactions
contrast_with: NFC (4 cm range) vs Bluetooth (10m) vs Wi-Fi (100m)
contemporary_relevance: NFC is the backbone of UPI-NFC and contactless payment systems in India.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Students may confuse Golan Heights with other conflict zones; it is specifically the disputed plateau between Israel and Syria in the Middle East.
elimination_hint: Golan Heights is a strategic plateau captured by Israel from Syria in 1967 Six-Day War — firmly Middle East geography.
factual_hooks: Golan Heights: strategic plateau between Israel and Syria
Captured by Israel in 1967, US recognized Israeli sovereignty in 2019
contrast_with: Golan Heights (Israel-Syria) vs West Bank (Israel-Palestine)
contemporary_relevance: US recognition of Israeli sovereignty over Golan Heights in 2019 changed international dynamics.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option A (convertibility = exchange for gold) is a classic trap referencing the gold standard which no longer applies; option B (market-determined exchange rate) is about floating rate, not convertibility per se.
elimination_hint: Convertibility means freely converting one currency to another without restrictions; it has two forms — current account (for trade) and capital account (for investments).
factual_hooks: Rupee convertibility means free conversion to/from other currencies
India has current account convertibility but restricted capital account convertibility
Full capital account convertibility remains a policy debate
contrast_with: Current account convertibility (achieved) vs capital account convertibility (partial)
contemporary_relevance: Debate on full capital account convertibility resurfaces periodically in Indian economic policy.

subject: History
trap_factor: Champaka and Kuluta are obscure medieval states; students may guess based on phonetic similarity rather than historical knowledge.
elimination_hint: Durgara is correctly identified with Jammu region; Champaka was in Bengal/Bihar area, not Central India; Kuluta was in Kullu valley (Himachal Pradesh), not Malabar.
factual_hooks: Durgara = Jammu region (medieval name)
Kuluta = Kullu Valley, Himachal Pradesh — not Malabar
Champaka = possibly Bihar/Bengal region
contrast_with: Durgara (Jammu) correctly matched vs Kuluta incorrectly placed in Malabar

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Vamsadhara and Pennar are often confused as Godavari tributaries; both actually flow independently into the Bay of Bengal, not into Godavari.
elimination_hint: Indravati and Pranahita are major left-bank tributaries of Godavari; Vamsadhara and Pennar are independent rivers, making the answer 2 and 3 only.
factual_hooks: Indravati and Pranahita are major Godavari tributaries
Vamsadhara flows independently into Bay of Bengal in Andhra/Odisha
Pennar is an independent river in Andhra Pradesh
contrast_with: Godavari tributaries vs independent peninsular rivers

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students often confuse joint sitting majority with special majority (2/3rd); the joint sitting requires only simple majority of members present and voting.
elimination_hint: Under Article 108, joint sitting passes bill by simple majority of total members present and voting — not two-thirds or absolute majority.
factual_hooks: Joint sitting (Article 108): simple majority of members present and voting
Joint sitting presided by Speaker of Lok Sabha
Only held 3 times in Indian parliamentary history
related_concepts: Money Bill — no joint sitting possible
contrast_with: Simple majority (joint sitting) vs Special majority (Constitutional amendment) vs Absolute majority

subject: Geography
trap_factor: South-West Bengal (Sundarbans) has mangroves and is a tempting wrong answer; but the combination of all three forest types is unique to Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
elimination_hint: Andaman & Nicobar Islands uniquely combine mangrove (coastal), tropical evergreen (interior), and some deciduous forests; no mainland region has all three together.
factual_hooks: Andaman & Nicobar Islands have mangrove + evergreen + deciduous forest combination
Sundarbans (West Bengal) has mangrove but not evergreen forests
contrast_with: Sundarbans (mainly mangrove) vs Andaman Islands (all three forest types)

subject: History
trap_factor: Avanti is sometimes associated with Buddha's life as monks went there; Gandhara (northwest) was not part of Buddha's main life journey in the Gangetic plain.
elimination_hint: Buddha's life was centred in Kosala (birthplace Lumbini, preached in Sravasti) and Magadha (Bodh Gaya, Rajgir); Avanti and Gandhara were peripheral to his main activities.
factual_hooks: Kosala and Magadha were primary kingdoms in Buddha's life
Avanti was associated with some disciples but not Buddha's main journey
Gandhara (northwest) had no direct connection to Buddha's life events
contrast_with: Kosala and Magadha (directly linked) vs Avanti (peripheral) vs Gandhara (not linked to Buddha's life)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Kyoto Protocol deals with greenhouse gases making it an attractive wrong answer; Nagoya Protocol deals with biodiversity access and benefit sharing, not ozone.
elimination_hint: Montreal Protocol (1987) specifically targets ozone-depleting substances like CFCs; Kyoto Protocol targets greenhouse gases; these are two distinct environmental agreements.
factual_hooks: Montreal Protocol 1987: controls ozone-depleting substances (CFCs, HCFCs, HBFCs)
Most successful multilateral environmental agreement — ozone layer recovering
Kigali Amendment 2016 extended Montreal Protocol to cover HFCs
related_concepts: Vienna Convention for Protection of Ozone Layer
contrast_with: Montreal Protocol (ozone) vs Kyoto Protocol (greenhouse gases) vs Nagoya Protocol (biodiversity)
contemporary_relevance: Kigali Amendment to Montreal Protocol addresses HFCs relevant to India's cooling sector.

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Structural unemployment arises from mismatch between skills demanded and available
PLFS (Periodic Labour Force Survey) is the current methodology for measuring unemployment in India
India uses Usual Status, Weekly Status, and Current Daily Status to measure employment
contrast_with: Cyclical unemployment (demand-driven) vs structural unemployment (skills mismatch)
mains_intro: India's unemployment challenge is primarily structural, rooted in skill gaps and sectoral imbalances that aggregate demand stimulus alone cannot address.
mains_body_points: Structural unemployment: agriculture-industry transition, automation, skill mismatch
PLFS methodology: Usual Principal Status, Weekly Status, Current Daily Status — limitations of each
Issues: underestimation of underemployment, exclusion of informal sector nuances, lack of real-time data
Improvements: more frequent surveys, better gig economy coverage, state-level granularity, longitudinal tracking
mains_conclusion: Reforming employment measurement to capture structural realities is essential for designing targeted skilling and industrial policies.
contemporary_relevance: India's demographic dividend turns into liability if structural unemployment is not addressed through skilling.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Finance Commission is associated with resource allocation and could be confused with planning; Law Commission is an advisory body students sometimes mix up.
elimination_hint: NITI Aayog replaced the Planning Commission in January 2015; Finance Commission and Law Commission continue to exist separately.
factual_hooks: NITI Aayog replaced Planning Commission on 1 January 2015
Planning Commission was established in 1950; NITI Aayog is a think tank with no fund allocation powers
related_concepts: Five Year Plans (replaced by NITI Aayog documents)
contrast_with: Planning Commission (fund allocation, directive planning) vs NITI Aayog (advisory, think tank)
contemporary_relevance: NITI Aayog's Aspirational Districts Programme reflects its advisory development role.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Rio+20 could be confused with UNFCCC COP meetings held near same period; it is specifically the UN Conference on Sustainable Development, not a climate change conference.
elimination_hint: Rio+20 (2012) was the UN Conference on Sustainable Development held in Rio de Janeiro — 20 years after the original Rio Earth Summit 1992.
factual_hooks: Rio+20 (2012): UN Conference on Sustainable Development in Rio de Janeiro
Produced 'The Future We Want' outcome document
Led to creation of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)
contrast_with: Rio+20 (sustainable development) vs UNFCCC COP (climate change) vs CBD COP (biodiversity)
contemporary_relevance: SDGs emerging from Rio+20 are India's key development framework.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Both statements seem plausible: executive power vested in PM is a common misconception (it is vested in President); PM as Chairman of Civil Services Board is also incorrect (Cabinet Secretary chairs it).
elimination_hint: Under Article 53, executive power is vested in the President, not PM. PM is ex officio Chairman of NITI Aayog, not Civil Services Board (Cabinet Secretary chairs it). Both statements are wrong.
factual_hooks: Executive power vested in President of India (Article 53), PM exercises it on President's behalf
PM is ex officio Chairman of NITI Aayog, not Civil Services Board
Civil Services Board is chaired by Cabinet Secretary
prerequisite_concepts: Role of President vs Prime Minister
contrast_with: Constitutional vesting of executive power (President) vs de facto executive (PM)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The term 'zone' might make students think of Earth's atmosphere; Goldilocks Zone is an astronomical concept related to planetary habitability in outer space.
elimination_hint: Goldilocks Zone (habitable zone) refers to the orbital range around a star where liquid water can exist — used in the search for Earth-like exoplanets.
factual_hooks: Goldilocks Zone: circumstellar habitable zone where liquid water can exist
Used to identify potentially habitable exoplanets around stars
Named after Goldilocks story — 'not too hot, not too cold'
contrast_with: Goldilocks Zone (habitability) vs Snow Line (water ice boundary in solar systems)
contemporary_relevance: James Webb Space Telescope is characterizing exoplanet atmospheres in habitable zones.

subject: History
trap_factor: V.O. Chidambaram Pillai is associated with the Swadeshi movement and coastal Tamil Nadu, which could confuse students into selecting him.
elimination_hint: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) organized a salt march from Tiruchengode to Vedaranyam on the Tanjore coast in April 1930 — separate from Gandhi's Dandi March.
factual_hooks: C. Rajagopalachari led salt march to Vedaranyam (Tanjore coast), April 1930
Parallel salt marches were organized across India alongside Gandhi's Dandi March
contrast_with: Gandhi's Dandi March (Gujarat coast) vs Rajagopalachari's Vedaranyam March (Tamil Nadu coast)

subject: History
trap_factor: Harihara I (founder of Vijayanagara) is the answer but students may confuse him with Amoghavarsha (Rashtrakuta) or Ballala II (Hoysala) as they are also Deccan rulers.
elimination_hint: Harihara I founded Vijayanagara on the south bank of Tungabhadra (tributary of Krishna) and ruled as agent of Virupaksha (deity). The description matches Vijayanagara Empire's founding mythology.
factual_hooks: Harihara I founded Vijayanagara Empire (1336) on Tungabhadra's south bank
Ruled as agent of deity Virupaksha — all land south of Krishna 'belonged' to the deity
Capital at Hampi (Vijayanagara city)
contrast_with: Harihara I (Vijayanagara founder) vs Amoghavarsha I (Rashtrakuta)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: BARC and TIFR are associated with nuclear science and Bhabha; students may confuse them with space research achievements.
elimination_hint: ISRO won the Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development 2014 for its Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) — India's first successful interplanetary mission.
factual_hooks: ISRO awarded Indira Gandhi Prize 2014 for successful Mars Orbiter Mission
Mangalyaan made India first country to succeed in Mars mission on first attempt
contrast_with: ISRO (space) vs BARC (nuclear) vs IISc (general science)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Khangchendzonga and Nandadevi are high-altitude parks with diverse climate zones, leading students to choose them over Namdapha in Arunachal Pradesh.
elimination_hint: Namdapha National Park in Arunachal Pradesh spans from tropical to alpine (arctic-like) due to its extreme altitude range from 200m to 4500m — the only park with all four climate zones.
factual_hooks: Namdapha NP, Arunachal Pradesh: tropical to subtropical to temperate to alpine
Altitude range 200m to 4571m gives it exceptional climatic diversity
Home to snow leopard, clouded leopard, tiger and elephant — unique assemblage
contrast_with: Namdapha (all 4 climate zones) vs Khangchendzonga (3 zones) vs Nandadevi (Himalayan only)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Students may confuse Amnesty International with UNHCR (UN refugee agency) or Médecins Sans Frontières (medical aid); Amnesty is specifically about human rights advocacy.
elimination_hint: Amnesty International is a global NGO focused on human rights — not a UN agency, not strictly for refugees or medical aid; it campaigns against torture, arbitrary detention, death penalty.
factual_hooks: Amnesty International: global human rights NGO founded 1961 by Peter Benenson
Nobel Peace Prize winner 1977
Campaigns against torture, death penalty, political prisoners
contrast_with: Amnesty International (NGO) vs UNHCR (UN body) vs Human Rights Council (inter-governmental)
contemporary_relevance: Amnesty International's reports on India's human rights situation are periodically cited in international forums.

subject: History
trap_factor: Lingaraja Temple and Varaha Image at Udayagiri are both ancient but students may confuse their chronology; Rock-cut monuments at Mahabalipuram are Pallava period (7th century CE) which seems old.
elimination_hint: The Rock-cut Elephant at Dhauli dates to Ashoka's period (3rd century BCE) — oldest among the options. Mahabalipuram is Pallava (7th century CE), Lingaraja is 11th century CE, Udayagiri is 5th century CE.
factual_hooks: Rock-cut Elephant at Dhauli: Ashokan period, 3rd century BCE — earliest
Udayagiri: Gupta period, 5th century CE
Mahabalipuram: Pallava, 7th–8th century CE
Lingaraja Temple: 11th century CE
prerequisite_concepts: Ancient Indian art and architecture timeline
contrast_with: Ashokan period (3rd BCE) vs Gupta (5th CE) vs Pallava (7th CE) vs Kalinga (11th CE)

subject: Society
trap_factor: This is a dated question (Census 2001 data); Maharashtra is the correct answer but West Bengal and Bihar are also large states causing confusion.
elimination_hint: As per Census 2001: UP (1st), Maharashtra (2nd), Bihar (3rd), West Bengal (4th) — Maharashtra is the second most populous state.
factual_hooks: Census 2001: UP > Maharashtra > Bihar > West Bengal in population
Maharashtra surpassed Bihar after 1991 census
contrast_with: Census 2001 rankings vs Census 2011 rankings (Maharashtra remains 2nd)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 is the key trap — feudalism is characterised by WEAK central authority and STRONG local lords, exactly the opposite of what statement 1 claims.
elimination_hint: Feudalism involves weak central authority with strong local lords (negates statement 1), land-based administrative structure (statement 2 correct), and lord-vassal relationships (statement 3 correct). Answer: 2 and 3 only.
factual_hooks: Feudalism: decentralized power, land grants (samantas), lord-vassal ties
Indian feudalism debated — D.D. Kosambi's model of 'feudalism from above and below'
Strong central authority is antithetical to feudal system
contrast_with: Feudalism (decentralized) vs Imperial systems (centralized)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: UNEP is associated with environment programs and could be confused as the manager; ADB manages development funds but not specifically forest carbon funds.
elimination_hint: BioCarbon Fund Initiative for Sustainable Forest Landscapes is managed by the World Bank — it is a World Bank climate finance initiative focusing on landscape-level emissions reductions.
factual_hooks: BioCarbon Fund Initiative for Sustainable Forest Landscapes managed by World Bank
Part of World Bank's climate finance portfolio
Supports jurisdictional approaches to REDD+
contrast_with: World Bank BioCarbon Fund vs UN-REDD Programme (UNDP/UNEP/FAO)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: India is often assumed to be part of APEC and ASEAN due to its economic engagement; India is only a dialogue partner of ASEAN and observer in APEC, not a member.
elimination_hint: India is a member of East Asia Summit (EAS) only among these three; it is not a member of APEC (21 members, Pacific rim) or ASEAN (10 Southeast Asian nations).
factual_hooks: India is member of East Asia Summit since its inception in 2005
India is not a member of APEC — not on Pacific rim
India is not a member of ASEAN — is a dialogue partner
contrast_with: ASEAN (10 SE Asian states) vs EAS (18 states including India) vs APEC (21 Pacific rim economies)
contemporary_relevance: India's engagement with EAS is central to its Act East Policy.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse between Brazil-Peru and Paraguay-Ecuador based on phonetic similarity of South American countries.
elimination_hint: Itaipu Dam on the Paraná River is a joint project of Brazil and Paraguay — it straddles their border and both countries share its electricity output.
factual_hooks: Itaipu Dam: Brazil-Paraguay joint hydroelectric project on River Paraná
One of world's largest hydroelectric dams by power generation
Paraguay gets 50% of electricity — exports most to Brazil
contrast_with: Itaipu (Brazil-Paraguay) vs Yacyretá (Argentina-Paraguay)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Saltpetre (used in explosives) and rock phosphate (fertilizers) are associated with other industries; students may include them, making 'all of the above' tempting.
elimination_hint: Indian steel industry specifically requires coking coal (for blast furnaces) which India lacks sufficiently and must import; saltpetre and rock phosphate are not steel industry requirements.
factual_hooks: India imports coking coal for steel production — domestic coking coal quality is poor
Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh have coal but insufficient coking coal quality
Australia is India's major coking coal import source
contrast_with: Coking coal (blast furnace) vs thermal coal (power generation) vs PCI coal
contemporary_relevance: India's steel industry import dependency on coking coal is a strategic vulnerability.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Fifth Schedule (Scheduled Areas) and Sixth Schedule (Tribal Areas) are both for tribes but students may confuse them with Panchayati Raj provisions (11th Schedule) or boundary provisions.
elimination_hint: Both Fifth Schedule (administration of Scheduled Areas) and Sixth Schedule (autonomous district councils in NE India) are specifically designed to protect Scheduled Tribes' interests.
factual_hooks: Fifth Schedule: administration and control of Scheduled Areas (mainland tribal areas)
Sixth Schedule: autonomous district councils in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram
Both protect Scheduled Tribes' interests, land rights, and customary laws
prerequisite_concepts: Fifth and Sixth Schedules of Constitution
contrast_with: Fifth Schedule (mainland) vs Sixth Schedule (Northeast tribal areas)
contemporary_relevance: Fifth and Sixth Schedule provisions are debated in context of mining projects in tribal areas.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may think all listed cities were former capitals; Brisbane was never a capital of Australia — Canberra is the current capital, Melbourne was the temporary capital before Canberra.
elimination_hint: Brisbane (Australia) was never a capital — it's the capital of Queensland state only. Karachi (Pakistan), Auckland (New Zealand) and Kyoto (Japan) were all former national capitals.
factual_hooks: Brisbane: capital of Queensland, never national capital of Australia
Karachi: Pakistan's first capital (1947-1958)
Auckland: New Zealand's capital until 1865 (Wellington replaced it)
Kyoto: Japan's imperial capital until 1869
contrast_with: Brisbane (state capital) vs Canberra (national capital) vs Melbourne (former temporary capital)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 traps those who confuse Department of Revenue with Department of Economic Affairs; Statement 3 traps those who think Public Account disbursements need parliamentary authorization.
elimination_hint: Union Budget is prepared by Dept of Economic Affairs, not Revenue (statement 1 wrong). CIF withdrawal needs Parliament's authorization (statement 2 correct). Public Account disbursements do NOT need parliamentary authorization as it is not government money (statement 3 wrong). Answer: 2 only.
factual_hooks: Union Budget prepared by Dept of Economic Affairs (Ministry of Finance)
Consolidated Fund: every withdrawal needs parliamentary authorization (Article 114)
Public Account: government acts as banker — no parliamentary authorization needed for withdrawals
prerequisite_concepts: Consolidated Fund vs Public Account vs Contingency Fund
related_concepts: Article 266 — Consolidated Fund and Public Account
contrast_with: Consolidated Fund (Parliament authorized) vs Public Account (no Parliament needed) vs Contingency Fund (executive used)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students confuse constitutional custodian with executive authority; President takes oath to protect constitution but Supreme Court is the ultimate constitutional custodian.
elimination_hint: The Supreme Court of India is the ultimate custodian/guardian of the Constitution — it interprets, protects, and upholds it; the President upholds but doesn't interpret it.
factual_hooks: Supreme Court: ultimate custodian and interpreter of Constitution of India
Judicial review power makes Supreme Court the guardian
President takes oath to protect Constitution but is not its interpreter
contrast_with: Supreme Court (interprets constitution) vs President (upholds constitution through oath)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 about AIBP incorrectly says it provides 'loan assistance to farmers'; it actually provides central assistance to complete major/medium irrigation projects.
elimination_hint: AIBP (1996-97) provides central loans to state governments for irrigation projects, not directly to poor farmers. Command Area Development Programme (1974-75) for water-use efficiency is correctly stated. Answer: 2 only.
factual_hooks: AIBP (Accelerated Irrigation Benefits Programme) 1996-97: central assistance to states for incomplete irrigation projects
Command Area Development Programme 1974-75: water-use efficiency from canal irrigation
AIBP target: complete long-pending major/medium irrigation projects
contrast_with: AIBP (project completion) vs CADP (water-use efficiency from operational projects)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Food Safety and Standards Act seems plausible as GEAC deals with GM food crops; Biological Diversity Act is also associated with biodiversity and genetic resources.
elimination_hint: GEAC is constituted under the Environment (Protection) Act 1986 — it is an environment regulation body, not a food safety or biodiversity body.
factual_hooks: GEAC (Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee) constituted under Environment Protection Act 1986
GEAC approves large-scale trials and environmental release of genetically engineered organisms
Under Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change
contrast_with: GEAC (Environment Act) vs FSSAI (Food Safety Act) for different aspects of GM food regulation
contemporary_relevance: GEAC's role in approving GM mustard is a contentious ongoing issue.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: China seems like a participant given 'Mekong' in the name; Bangladesh is sometimes confused due to India-Bangladesh-Myanmar connectivity discussions.
elimination_hint: Mekong-Ganga Cooperation has 6 members: India, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam. Bangladesh and China are NOT members — China controls upper Mekong but is not in MGC.
factual_hooks: MGC members: India, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam (6 countries)
Bangladesh and China are NOT members of MGC
MGC focuses on tourism, culture, education, transport in the Mekong-Ganga region
contrast_with: MGC (6 states, no China) vs Greater Mekong Subregion (includes China)
contemporary_relevance: MGC is part of India's Act East Policy to enhance connectivity with Southeast Asia.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Basel III sounds environmental due to 'reducing burden on developed countries' option (C); the Nagoya/Basel confusion on biodiversity convention is also common.
elimination_hint: Basel III is a banking regulation framework (not environmental); it strengthens bank capital requirements, liquidity standards, and leverage ratios to prevent financial crises.
factual_hooks: Basel III: international banking regulatory framework by Basel Committee on Banking Supervision
Strengthens capital adequacy, liquidity coverage ratio, leverage ratio
Developed after 2008 global financial crisis
contrast_with: Basel III (banking) vs Basel Convention (hazardous waste) — same name, different domains
contemporary_relevance: Basel III implementation is central to RBI's banking sector reform agenda.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 has a geography error — westerlies blow between 30-60 degrees but in BOTH hemispheres (not 30°N to 60°S which spans both); the latitude range given mixes N and S incorrectly.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong — westerlies blow 30-60° in each hemisphere (30-60°N and 30-60°S separately, not mixed). Statement 2 is correct — western disturbances affecting NW India are moist air masses embedded in the westerlies.
factual_hooks: Westerlies: 30-60° latitude in each hemisphere (not mixed N-S as stated in Q)
Western disturbances: extra-tropical cyclones embedded in westerlies bringing winter rains to NW India
NW India's winter rains (December-February) from westerlies originating over Mediterranean
contrast_with: Westerlies (mid-latitudes) vs Trade winds (tropical) vs Polar easterlies (polar regions)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: IOR-ARC (now IORA) was not 'recently' established (it was set up in 1997) and is not limited to maritime security — both statements are wrong making 'neither' the answer.
elimination_hint: IOR-ARC established in 1997 (not recently in 2015 context). It covers trade, investment, tourism, science — not just maritime security. Both statements are incorrect.
factual_hooks: IOR-ARC (now IORA) established in 1997 in Mauritius
Covers trade facilitation, science/technology, tourism, disaster management — not just maritime security
Renamed Indian Ocean Rim Association (IORA) in 2013
contrast_with: IORA (broad mandate) vs Indian Ocean Naval Symposium (maritime security only)
contemporary_relevance: India's IORA chairmanship (2011-2013) elevated its Indian Ocean leadership role.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Jharkhand has tribal traditions with forests but no living root bridges; Himachal Pradesh has tree bridges concept but not the specific Ficus living root bridges.
elimination_hint: Living root bridges are made from the aerial roots of Ficus elastica trees by the Khasi and Jaintia tribes — found exclusively in Meghalaya's high-rainfall forest regions.
factual_hooks: Living root bridges: created by Khasi and Jaintia tribes of Meghalaya using Ficus elastica roots
Found in Cherrapunji and Mawlynnong areas of Meghalaya
UNESCO recognized them as part of intangible cultural heritage
contrast_with: Meghalaya living root bridges (unique) vs bamboo bridges elsewhere in India

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may think only Moon's gravity causes tides; all three forces — Sun's gravity, Moon's gravity, and Earth's centrifugal force — contribute to tidal phenomena.
elimination_hint: Tides result from differential gravitational pull of Moon (primary) and Sun (secondary) plus centrifugal force from Earth-Moon system rotation — all three factors are correct.
factual_hooks: Moon's gravity: primary cause of tides (closer proximity)
Sun's gravity: secondary cause (larger mass but greater distance)
Centrifugal force of Earth-Moon rotation creates tidal bulge on opposite side
contrast_with: Lunar tides (stronger) vs Solar tides (weaker) vs their combined effect

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: IRS satellites are remote sensing satellites — telecommunications and traffic studies require different satellite types; students may assume IRS covers all applications.
elimination_hint: IRS (Indian Remote Sensing) satellites use optical and microwave sensors for earth observation — crop assessment, groundwater, minerals are appropriate uses; telecom and traffic studies are not typical remote sensing applications.
factual_hooks: IRS satellites used for: crop productivity, groundwater, mineral exploration, disaster monitoring, land use
Telecommunications use geostationary INSAT/GSAT satellites, not IRS
Traffic studies use ground sensors, not remote sensing satellites
contrast_with: IRS (earth observation/remote sensing) vs INSAT/GSAT (telecom and meteorology)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Himachal Pradesh (temperate, not tropical) and Mizoram (tropical but with some evergreen) — Mizoram does have tropical wet evergreen forests but Himachal Pradesh does not.
elimination_hint: Tropical Wet Evergreen Forests require high rainfall and warm temperatures — Arunachal Pradesh (Eastern Himalayan foothills) and Mizoram (high rainfall NE India) qualify; Himachal Pradesh is temperate/alpine.
factual_hooks: Tropical Wet Evergreen Forests: Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram (and other NE states) have them
Himachal Pradesh: temperate/alpine forests, NOT tropical wet evergreen
Require >200cm annual rainfall and warm temperatures
contrast_with: Tropical wet evergreen (NE India, Western Ghats) vs Temperate forests (Himachal Pradesh)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Options mention radar system and Antarctic — IndARC sounds like an Arctic mooring system but students may confuse with Antarctic station (Maitri/Bharati).
elimination_hint: IndARC is India's first underwater observatory/mooring system deployed in the Arctic Ocean to study ocean dynamics and climate — specifically in the Arctic, not Antarctic.
factual_hooks: IndARC: India's underwater observatory in Kongsfjorden, Arctic region (2014)
Studies ocean temperature, salinity, current dynamics in Arctic
Part of India's Arctic research programme from Himadri station
related_concepts: National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research
contrast_with: IndARC (Arctic underwater) vs Maitri/Bharati (Antarctic surface stations)
contemporary_relevance: Arctic research is gaining importance as climate change opens the Arctic Ocean.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the key trap — Forest Carbon Partnership Facility does NOT fund universities/researchers; it specifically supports developing country governments for REDD+ readiness.
elimination_hint: FCPF is a global partnership of governments, businesses, and civil society (statement 1 correct). It assists developing countries with REDD+ financial and technical support (statement 3 correct). Statement 2 about funding universities is wrong.
factual_hooks: Forest Carbon Partnership Facility (FCPF): World Bank-hosted, global partnership
Supports developing countries with REDD+ readiness and carbon fund payments
Does NOT fund academic research — focuses on government capacity building
contrast_with: FCPF (country REDD+ support) vs UN-REDD (technical assistance to governments) vs research funding bodies
contemporary_relevance: REDD+ and forest carbon are increasingly important in India's climate finance discussions.

subject: History
trap_factor: Konkani and Assamese are regional languages students may associate with classical status campaigns; Odia received classical language status in 2014.
elimination_hint: Odia (Oriya) was granted classical language status in 2014, making it the 6th classical language after Tamil, Sanskrit, Telugu, Kannada, and Malayalam.
factual_hooks: Odia granted classical language status in 2014 — 6th classical language of India
Criteria: antiquity >1000 years, ancient literature, distinct from modern language
Tamil (2004) was first classical language recognized
contrast_with: Odia (classical since 2014) vs Konkani/Bhojpuri (not classical languages)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 2 is a trap — 'biodiversity hotspots' concept originated from Norman Myers (not BirdLife International); BirdLife identifies IBAs, not hotspots.
elimination_hint: BirdLife International is a global conservation partnership (statement 1 correct) that identifies Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas (statement 3 correct). The biodiversity hotspots concept was Norman Myers' contribution, not BirdLife's (statement 2 wrong).
factual_hooks: BirdLife International: global partnership of conservation organizations with national partners
Identifies Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas (IBAs) globally
Biodiversity hotspots concept: Norman Myers (1988), adopted by Conservation International — not BirdLife
contrast_with: BirdLife IBAs (Important Bird Areas) vs Conservation International hotspots

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Jordan is in Southwest Asia and borders Israel making students think it has Mediterranean access; Jordan is landlocked except for its tiny Red Sea (Gulf of Aqaba) coast.
elimination_hint: Jordan does not border the Mediterranean Sea — it is landlocked except for its small Red Sea coast at Aqaba. Syria, Lebanon, and Israel all have Mediterranean coastlines.
factual_hooks: Jordan: landlocked on Mediterranean side — only sea access is Gulf of Aqaba (Red Sea)
Syria, Lebanon, Israel all have Mediterranean coastlines
Aqaba is Jordan's only port city
contrast_with: Lebanon (Mediterranean) vs Jordan (only Red Sea/Gulf of Aqaba access)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Both statements appear plausible; the Legislative Council CAN be larger than 1/3 of Legislative Assembly (NOT half) and Chairman is NOT nominated by Governor but elected by Council members.
elimination_hint: LC cannot exceed 1/3rd of LA membership (not half — statement 1 says 'larger than half' which makes this wrong). Chairman of LC is elected by LC members, NOT nominated by Governor (statement 2 wrong). Answer: Neither.
factual_hooks: Legislative Council: cannot exceed 1/3rd of Legislative Assembly strength
Chairman of Legislative Council: elected by Council members (not nominated by Governor)
Governor nominates 1/6th of LC members
prerequisite_concepts: Composition and powers of State Legislature
related_concepts: Rajya Sabha composition vs Legislative Council composition
contrast_with: LC Chairman (elected by members) vs Governor-nominated members (1/6th of LC)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse teak with rainforest trees or choose tropical dry deciduous when teak is actually in tropical moist deciduous forests.
elimination_hint: Teak (Tectona grandis) is the dominant species of Tropical Moist Deciduous Forests — found in areas with 100-200cm rainfall; not in rainforests (too wet) or thorn scrub (too dry).
factual_hooks: Teak dominates Tropical Moist Deciduous Forests in India
Found in Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Karnataka at 100-200cm rainfall zones
India is one of the world's largest teak producers
prerequisite_concepts: Forest types of India by rainfall
contrast_with: Tropical moist deciduous (teak) vs tropical dry deciduous (less rainfall) vs tropical rainforest (>200cm)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Beijing Declaration sounds geopolitical so students may associate it with Shanghai Cooperation Organization or East Asia Summit rather than a UN women's conference.
elimination_hint: Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action (1995) was the outcome of the 4th World Conference on Women convened by the United Nations — it is a global agenda for women's empowerment.
factual_hooks: Beijing Declaration 1995: 4th UN World Conference on Women outcome
Platform for Action: comprehensive agenda covering 12 critical areas of women's concerns
25th anniversary reviewed as Beijing+25 in 2020
contrast_with: Beijing Declaration (women's rights) vs Beijing Declaration on Tibet/China (geopolitical)
contemporary_relevance: Beijing+25 review process shapes current global gender equality agenda.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Monsoon climate also has afternoon rains; the key clues — predictable daily pattern, thunderstorms, rain is 'soon over' — point to equatorial convectional rainfall, not monsoon.
elimination_hint: The description of morning clear sky, afternoon convectional thunderstorm, brief heavy rain — is the classic equatorial climate pattern; monsoon rains are more prolonged and seasonal.
factual_hooks: Equatorial climate: morning calm, afternoon convectional thunderstorms, brief heavy rain daily
Savannah has distinct wet and dry seasons — not daily predictable pattern
Monsoon rains last hours to days, not 'soon over'
contrast_with: Equatorial (daily convectional) vs Monsoon (seasonal orographic/frontal) vs Savannah (wet-dry seasons)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may assume both growth rate and nominal GDP increased steadily; real GDP growth rate has NOT been monotonically increasing — it fluctuated significantly in the last decade.
elimination_hint: GDP at market prices in rupees (nominal) steadily increases due to both growth and inflation — statement 2 correct. Real GDP growth rate fluctuates (fell during 2012-13 slowdown, COVID) — statement 1 wrong.
factual_hooks: Nominal GDP in rupees always increases due to nominal effects (inflation + growth)
Real GDP growth rate fluctuates — India had slowdowns in 2012-14 and COVID-19 contraction in 2020-21
Real growth rate ≠ steady increase over any decade
contrast_with: Nominal GDP (always rises in rupees) vs Real GDP growth rate (fluctuates)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may not know IREDA's dual status; it being both a Public Limited Company AND an NBFC simultaneously is the correct answer.
elimination_hint: IREDA is a Public Limited Government Company (under Companies Act) AND also classified as an NBFC (Non-Banking Financial Company) by RBI — both statements are correct.
factual_hooks: IREDA: Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency Limited — Government of India NBFC
Provides term loans for renewable energy and energy efficiency projects
Both a public company and NBFC — listed on stock exchanges
contrast_with: IREDA (renewable energy NBFC) vs PFC (power finance) vs REC (rural electrification)
contemporary_relevance: IREDA's IPO (2023) highlighted its role in financing India's renewable energy targets.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Public debt and public revenue sound like monetary terms but they are fiscal policy tools; only bank rate and open market operations are RBI's monetary policy instruments.
elimination_hint: Monetary policy instruments include bank rate, repo rate, open market operations, CRR, SLR — all RBI tools. Public debt and public revenue are government fiscal instruments, not monetary policy.
factual_hooks: Monetary policy instruments: bank rate, repo rate, OMOs, CRR, SLR — all RBI tools
Public debt and public revenue are fiscal policy tools (government budget)
RBI's monetary policy targets inflation and money supply
contrast_with: Monetary policy (RBI) vs Fiscal policy (Government/Ministry of Finance)
contemporary_relevance: RBI's Monetary Policy Committee uses bank rate and OMOs as key policy tools.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option A says government alone controls inflation (ignoring RBI) and option D says increased money circulation helps — both are wrong but plausible-sounding to students.
elimination_hint: Inflation is controlled by reducing money supply (contractionary policy); decreased money circulation (through higher interest rates, OMO sales) helps control inflation — option C is correct.
factual_hooks: Inflation control: reducing money supply through higher interest rates, CRR hike, OMO bond sales
Both RBI (monetary) and Government (fiscal) have roles in inflation control
Quantitative theory of money: inflation rises with money supply
contrast_with: Contractionary monetary policy (controls inflation) vs expansionary policy (stimulates growth)
contemporary_relevance: RBI's flexible inflation targeting (4% ±2%) is the current framework for managing Indian inflation.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may confuse capital-augmenting with labour-augmenting; replacing wooden ploughs with steel increases capital productivity per unit, not labour productivity.
elimination_hint: Capital-augmenting technological progress increases the productivity/effectiveness of capital (the plough/tool); steel ploughs do more with same capital input — this is capital-augmenting progress.
factual_hooks: Capital-augmenting tech progress: same capital produces more output (better quality tools)
Labour-augmenting: same labour produces more output (e.g. mechanization)
Steel plough replacing wooden = improvement in capital quality = capital-augmenting
contrast_with: Capital-augmenting (better tools) vs Labour-augmenting (better use of human effort)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Lok Sabha being directly elected seems like the defining feature; Parliament amending the Constitution also seems fundamental — but parliamentary system is specifically defined by executive accountability.
elimination_hint: Parliamentary system's core feature is that the executive (Council of Ministers) is collectively responsible to/accountable to the Lok Sabha — this is what distinguishes it from presidential system.
factual_hooks: Parliamentary system: Council of Ministers collectively responsible to Lok Sabha (Article 75)
Direct election of Lok Sabha and Parliament amending power exist in both parliamentary and presidential systems
Council of Ministers loses power if it loses Lok Sabha confidence
contrast_with: Parliamentary system (executive accountable to legislature) vs Presidential system (executive independent)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: H1N1 is sometimes confused with H5N1 (bird flu); H1N1 is Swine Flu (influenza A) not bird flu.
elimination_hint: H1N1 is the influenza A subtype responsible for swine flu (2009 pandemic); H5N1 is the bird flu strain — these are different influenza subtypes.
factual_hooks: H1N1: Swine Flu — caused 2009 influenza pandemic
H5N1: Bird Flu (Avian Influenza)
Both are influenza A subtypes but different hemagglutinin and neuraminidase combinations
contrast_with: H1N1 (Swine Flu) vs H5N1 (Bird Flu) vs H3N2 (seasonal flu)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 is a trap — bio-toilets use a bacterial inoculum (anaerobic bacteria), not fungal; Statement 2 is wrong because CO2 and methane are also released, not just ammonia and water.
elimination_hint: Bio-toilets: decomposition by anaerobic bacteria (not fungi) — statement 1 wrong. End products include CO2, methane, ammonia, water — not just ammonia and water — statement 2 wrong. Answer: Neither.
factual_hooks: Bio-toilets use psychrophilic anaerobic bacteria (not fungi) to decompose waste
End products: CO2, methane, ammonia, water — carbon dioxide and methane are also produced
Technology developed by DRDO for Indian Railways
contrast_with: Aerobic decomposition (CO2 + H2O) vs Anaerobic (methane + CO2 + ammonia)
contemporary_relevance: Indian Railways deployed bio-toilets under Swachh Rail-Swachh Bharat initiative.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option B (gold and silver) is a classic trap referencing the historical gold standard; the modern international liquidity problem is about hard currencies not being available.
elimination_hint: International liquidity refers to the availability of internationally accepted currencies (dollars, euros, etc.) for settling international transactions; gold standard no longer applies.
factual_hooks: International liquidity: availability of hard currencies (USD, EUR, GBP) for international payments
SDRs (Special Drawing Rights) were created by IMF to supplement international liquidity
Countries with insufficient reserves face balance of payments crises
contrast_with: International liquidity (currency availability) vs domestic liquidity (money supply)
contemporary_relevance: India's forex reserves adequacy is evaluated against international liquidity norms.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Fuel cells do produce electricity but students may assume it's AC (like power plants); fuel cells produce DC which must be converted. Statement 2 (not for laptops) is also wrong.
elimination_hint: Pure hydrogen fuel cells emit only heat and water (statement 1 correct). Fuel cells CAN power small devices (statement 2 wrong). Fuel cells produce DC, not AC (statement 3 wrong). Answer: 1 only.
factual_hooks: Hydrogen fuel cell byproducts: heat and water (clean technology)
Fuel cells produce DC (Direct Current), not AC
Fuel cells power devices from laptops to buildings — not limited to buildings
contrast_with: Fuel cells (DC, electrochemical) vs generators (AC, mechanical) vs batteries (DC, electrochemical)
contemporary_relevance: Green hydrogen and fuel cells are central to India's National Hydrogen Mission.

subject: History
trap_factor: Other art forms (block prints, silk paintings) are associated with different regions; Kalamkari is specifically hand-painted (and block-printed) cotton textile from South India.
elimination_hint: Kalamkari (kalam = pen, kari = work) is a hand-painted or block-printed cotton textile art from Andhra Pradesh (Machilipatnam and Srikalahasti styles) in South India.
factual_hooks: Kalamkari: hand-painted/block-printed cotton textile from Andhra Pradesh, South India
Two styles: Machilipatnam (block printing) and Srikalahasti (hand drawing)
GI-tagged product; uses natural dyes
contrast_with: Kalamkari (South India, cotton) vs Madhubani (Bihar, paintings on paper/walls) vs Phulkari (Punjab, embroidery)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option A (using own farm seeds) sounds self-reliant and attractive but it focuses on auto-consumption; the Seed Village Concept is about trained production and supply to others.
elimination_hint: Seed Village Concept: trains farmers in quality seed production so they become local seed suppliers to other farmers at affordable cost and at the right time — option B is correct.
factual_hooks: Seed Village Concept: train farmers to produce quality certified seeds and supply them locally
Reduces dependence on distant seed companies; ensures timely availability
Part of National Seed Policy for decentralized seed production
contrast_with: Seed Village Concept (trained local production) vs contract farming with seed companies
contemporary_relevance: Seed village concept gains importance for ensuring food security and farmer income.
